comments.text: Let $f(n)$ be the maximum number of edges in a graph on $n$ vertices in which no two cycles have the same length. In 1975, Erd\H os raised the problem of determining $f(n)$ (see J.A. Bondy and U.S.R. Murty [ Graph Theory with Applications (Macmillan, New York, 1976).], p.247, Problem 11). Let $g(n)$ be the maximum number edges in an $n$-vertex, Hamiltonian graph with no repeated cycle length. Then $f(N) \geq g(N),$ for any integer $M$, there is $N \geq M$ such that $g(N) \geq h(N).$ also see E. Boros, Y. Caro, Z. F\"uredi and R. Yuster [ Covering non-uniform hypergraphs, J. Combin. Theory Ser. B 82(2001), 270-284.] also see Chapter 1 In book: Some Problems on Paths and Cycles https://www.researchgate.net/publication/392767794_Some_Problems_on_Paths_and_Cycles

I have been experimenting with computational approaches to Erdős Problem #30 (Sidon constant bounds) and recently attempted to improve the best known constant. Our current certified candidate gives: h(N) ≤ N^{1/2} + 0.98173 N^{1/4} + O(1), which slightly improves the previous record (C = 0.98183). I put together an open search + certification pipeline, and all code, data, and certificate bundles are available here 👉 https://github.com/rynald0cst0ltziam/Erdos-Sidon-Records-30 Feedback, independent verification, and discussion are very welcome.

Just a heads up that I am working with a group (Carter, Georgiev, Gomez–Serrano, Hunter, O’Bryant, Wagner) to improve the constant further. We were able to get 0.97633 in an unpublished work by optimizing the [CHO25] argument using AlphaEvolve, but have since found a more efficient argument that gives better bounds (tentatively 0.947, subject to confirmation). Details will hopefully appear soon.
The optimal $N^{1/4}$ coefficient in the upper bound for $h(N)$ is now recorded as $C_5$ in the optimization problems repository.
the bound of O'Bryant was further refined in a paper by Carter, myself, and O'Bryant: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.20032. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The initial upper bound of $N^{1/2} + N^{1/4} + 1$ (which stood for about half a century) was not only proven by Erdős-Turán and Lindström. The exact same bound was also arrived at in three more ways by Ruzsa, Graham and Cilleruelo. I wrote about them in details in this blog post. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
